planting.note: Direct sow in fall for cold stratification and spring germination; or divide established clumps in early spring. White avens self-sows reliably in moist, shaded woodland gardens and naturalizes readily once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; white avens is a common but underappreciated native woodland perennial of rich forest floors and moist woodland edges across eastern North America. The small, five-petaled white flowers on branching stems in early summer are modest in size but individually elegant, producing burr-like seed heads with long, hooked styles that cling to passing animals for dispersal. The basal foliage consists of pinnately compound leaves with a large, rounded terminal leaflet — fresh green and attractive throughout the growing season. White avens self-sows freely in moist woodland soils to fill gaps in the native groundcover layer, functioning as both an ornamental and a highly effective colonizer of bare shaded ground. An excellent low-growing, self-sustaining native for difficult shaded spots where other plants struggle to establish from seed.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, average to humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; tall hairy agrimony grows naturally at woodland edges, along fence rows, roadsides, and in open, brushy habitats throughout eastern North America. Division in spring is the most reliable propagation method.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; tall hairy agrimony is the most common of the native North American agrimonies — a tall, erect perennial with pinnately compound leaves and slender, tapering spikes of small yellow flowers throughout summer. The entire plant is covered in soft, spreading hairs (glandular on the stems), giving it a pleasantly tactile, soft-textured appearance. The distinctive burr-like fruits with hooked hooks cling readily to clothing and animal fur — a classic hitchhiker in the summer garden. Like European agrimony (A. eupatoria), the native species has a long history in traditional American herbal medicine as an astringent herb used for throat conditions, wounds, and digestive complaints. The dried herbage makes a pleasant, slightly astringent, aromatic tea. An excellent low-maintenance native for naturalized, semi-wild garden areas and hedgerow plantings.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants after last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; peanut butter fruit is more shade-tolerant than most tropical fruit shrubs and can produce fruit in filtered light, making it useful for partially shaded garden positions in zone 9–11.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in zones 9–12; peanut butter fruit earns its name from the distinctive flavor of the ripe orange-red fruits — the flesh surrounding the seeds has a rich, somewhat sticky texture and flavor uncannily reminiscent of peanut butter, combined with subtle sweet notes. Native to Peru and the Andes, this ornamental shrub is surprisingly adaptable to Gulf Coast conditions in zones 9–11, where it fruits prolifically on an almost continuous basis in warm seasons. The small, bright yellow flowers are attractive and produced repeatedly throughout the year in warm climates. The fruit are eaten fresh from the shrub or used in smoothies, ice cream, and desserts throughout South America. Unusually shade-tolerant for a fruiting tropical — it can produce reasonably well in the bright filtered light beneath a high canopy, making it valuable for partially shaded garden positions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; common cinquefoil spreads by long, horizontal stolons (runners) that root at the nodes to form expanding colonies in dry, open conditions. It grows naturally in dry open woods, sandy roadsides, and meadow edges.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; common cinquefoil is a native low-growing groundcover of dry, sandy, open habitats across eastern North America, spreading by long, slender stolons (runners) to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat of small, five-fingered (cinquefoil = 'five leaves') compound leaves. The small, bright yellow, five-petaled flowers bloom in spring and continue sporadically throughout summer. It is extremely drought-tolerant once established and thrives in lean, sandy, nutrient-poor soils where more demanding groundcovers fail. The spreading stolons root readily to stabilize sandy slopes and disturbed, dry banks. An excellent low-maintenance native groundcover for dry, sunny areas between stepping stones, along driveways, and in hell strips where lawn grass struggles. Unlike many cinquefoils, this species is not aggressive in richer soils.

planting.note: Plant grafted or air-layered container plants in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; chempedak is very frost-sensitive and is primarily for South Florida and tropical areas. Grafted plants begin fruiting at 3–5 years vs. 10+ years from seed.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; chempedak is a close relative of jackfruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus), native to Southeast Asia from Indonesia to Thailand, producing large, barrel-shaped compound fruits borne directly on the trunk and main branches like jackfruit but typically smaller and sweeter. The fruits have a strongly aromatic, intensely sweet, almost fermented flavor that is more pronounced than jackfruit — considered by many Southeast Asian aficionados to be superior to jackfruit for fresh eating. The soft, yellow-orange arils surrounding the seeds are eaten fresh, fried in batter (a popular street food in Malaysia), or incorporated into desserts. The seeds are roasted or boiled and eaten. More tolerant of wet, poorly drained soils than jackfruit, making it suitable for sites that jackfruit would struggle in. An outstanding choice for tropical South Florida gardens seeking an alternative to the more common jackfruit.
